Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Samuel Butler

1835 – 1902

Memory, habit, and the evolutionary continuity of mind and life.

I am treating “Samuel Butler” as the Victorian Samuel Butler, 1835–1902: the author of Erewhon, Life and Habit, Unconscious Memory, God the Known and God the Unknown, Luck, or Cunning?, Erewhon Revisited, and the Note-Books. The most defensible reconstruction is this: Butler’s philosophy of mind is a mnemic, embodied, anti-Cartesian, teleological, distributed, and ultimately theological theory of soul/mind. Mind is not, for him, simply reflective consciousness. It is the inward, purposive, memory-bearing side of organized matter; “soul” is not a detachable immaterial ghost, but the living, organizing, remembering, striving aspect of a body or body-like system. This interpretation is not imposed from outside: Butler repeatedly treats mind/matter, soul/body, heredity/memory, habit/instinct, organism/person, and God/world as different levels of one problem. The prefatory note to God the Known and God the Unknown explicitly says that work is closely allied to Life and Habit and Evolution, Old and New, while Butler’s later evolutionary essays are described as a postscript to Life and Habit, Evolution, Old and New, Unconscious Memory, and Luck, or Cunning?

Butler’s style is often satirical, polemical, and deliberately paradoxical, so a rigorous account must distinguish doctrine from provocation. Yet the core claims are stable across his serious evolutionary works, his theological essay, his fiction, and his notebooks. Life and Habit begins by framing the inquiry around unconscious or quasi-unconscious action, embryological development, instinct, and the continuation of life, while Butler also warns that he is writing for a general public rather than as a narrowly technical scientist. That combination matters: the theory is philosophically serious, but not formulated as a modern laboratory theory.

1. The central ontology: mind and body are two inseparable aspects

Butler’s fundamental metaphysical move is to deny that mind and body are two separable substances. In Unconscious Memory, he says that instinct and organization are “mind and body” or “mind and matter,” not two separable things, but one reality with two sides; he also says there is no matter without mind, however low, and no mind without some material body. This is the key to the whole system. Butler is not a Cartesian dualist, because he refuses a disembodied mind. He is not a reductive materialist, because he refuses mindless matter. He is closest to a dual-aspect monism, with a strong panpsychist or panzoistic inflection.

The Note-Books make the same point in a more aphoristic idiom. Butler says we cannot form a definite idea of pure matter without smuggling in some mental attribute, nor of pure mind without smuggling in some matter. He describes energy as a kind of soul, and says matter cannot be conceived except as capable of working power or, in that sense, as “ensouled.” Elsewhere he calls “eternal matter permeated by eternal mind” the least uncomfortable way of looking at the universe, while admitting that the whole matter exceeds comprehension.

This yields a crucial interpretive rule. Whenever Butler speaks of “soul,” he usually means the active, organizing, energetic, purposive aspect of a material system. Whenever he speaks of “mind,” he usually means more than conscious thought: he means the ability of organized matter to retain, repeat, adapt, express, and purposively carry forward its own history.

2. Consciousness is not the essence of mind

Butler’s most important contribution to philosophy of mind may be his reversal of the usual hierarchy between consciousness and unconsciousness. For him, consciousness is not the highest or most essential form of mentality. Consciousness appears when there is novelty, difficulty, uncertainty, hesitation, or incomplete mastery. When knowledge becomes perfect, it tends to become unconscious. In Life and Habit, he argues that we are conscious of knowing or willing chiefly when there is attention, suspense, doubt, and partial ignorance; by contrast, acquired mastery sinks into unconscious knowledge and unconscious volition.

This is why Butler’s “unconscious” is not primarily a Freudian unconscious of repression, though later readers could connect him to broader nineteenth-century discussions of unconscious process. It is closer to what we would now call skilled, procedural, embodied, or dispositional knowledge. To know how to walk, digest, breathe, grow, or develop embryologically is not, for Butler, to lack mind. It is to possess mind in a more ancient, habituated, and automatic form. The less a living thing seems to know what it is doing, the more this may show that it has done it so often that the knowledge no longer needs reflective supervision.

This gives Butler a distinctive test for mentality. The question is not primarily, “Can this being introspect?” or “Can it report conscious experience?” The question is, “Does this being act from retained experience, habit, purposive adjustment, and practical familiarity?” If yes, Butler is prepared to attribute mind, in some degree.

3. Memory is the substance of life

Butler’s mature philosophy of mind is a philosophy of memory. He does not treat memory merely as one mental faculty among others. Memory is the bond that unifies consciousness, the ground of habit, the basis of development, and the principle that makes heredity intelligible. In Life and Habit, he goes so far as to say that life is memory, that plants and animals differ because they remember different things, and that shape itself is a kind of memory or embodied idea of past history. Matter that can remember is, for Butler, living matter.

The Hering material included in Unconscious Memory gives a physiological version of the same thesis: memory is described as a primary power of organized matter and as the unifying bond of conscious life. It is not restricted to deliberate recollection. Habit, motor skill, the sympathetic ganglionic system, muscles, organs, and the inheritance of acquired tendencies are all brought under a broadened conception of memory.

This is the root of Butler’s philosophy of mind: mind is not a punctual act of awareness; it is organized retention. A mind is a living history that can repeat, vary, and apply itself. A body is not a dead container for mental life; it is the outward form taken by accumulated memory. This is why Butler can say in Luck, or Cunning? that bodily form may be understood as idea and memory solidified.

4. Heredity is unconscious memory

Butler’s most famous and most controversial claim is that heredity is a mode of memory. The editor’s introduction to Unconscious Memory summarizes Life and Habit in four main principles: the oneness of personality between parent and offspring; the offspring’s memory of actions performed in the persons of its forebears; the latency of such memory until rekindled by associated conditions; and the unconsciousness with which habitual actions are performed. A fifth principle is added: the purposiveness of living beings and of the machines they make or select.

In this view, an embryo develops as it does because it “remembers” the ancestral route of development. Instinct is inherited knowledge. The chick pecks at grain not as a blind mechanism, but as an organism drawing on ancestral experience. Hering’s lecture, translated and promoted by Butler, says that offspring reproduce processes in which the germ took part, and that a line of organisms transmits memory and acquired powers through descent.

This produces one of Butler’s strangest but most central doctrines: the embryo is a kind of cognitive agent. Butler even allows for “clever” and “stupid” embryos and interprets hybrid development as a conflict or fusion of inherited memories. The point is not that embryos deliberate like adult humans. The point is that development is not, for him, mere external mechanism. It is practical, inherited, unconscious knowing.

As a literal biological theory, Butler’s heredity-as-memory doctrine cannot simply be imported into contemporary genetics without major revision. Philosophically, however, it is powerful because it makes mind, body, habit, inheritance, and action continuous. Mentality is no longer a late addition to life. Life itself is already proto-mental because life is organized, retained, and self-repeating experience.

5. Instinct is not blind mechanism but inherited intelligence

Butler’s theory of instinct is a direct consequence of the heredity-memory thesis. In Unconscious Memory, he criticizes theories that personify “instinct” as if it were a separate agency acting through animals. For Butler, it is the animal that acts. Instinct is inherited knowledge embodied in organization, not a mysterious impersonal faculty.

He also denies that instinct is infallible. Instinctive actions can be corrected through practice; animals vary their actions, make mistakes, feel pleasure and pain, and learn. New or unfamiliar circumstances awaken consciousness and deliberation. This is exactly what one would expect if instinct were inherited habit: habit works best in familiar contexts and becomes conscious when disrupted.

This is a sophisticated philosophy of skill. The highest form of competence is not explicit rule-following but intelligently inherited and embodied fluency. Conscious thought is real and important, but it is not the foundation. It is an emergency or developmental layer built on older unconscious competencies.

6. Personal identity is continuous, porous, and distributed

Butler’s philosophy of mind also contains a radical account of personal identity. He does not ground identity in a simple ego. He grounds it in continuity without abrupt break. In Life and Habit, he treats parent and offspring as one continuous identity, using the egg-and-fowl sequence to show how identity can persist through transformations. Sexual reproduction complicates the matter further: the ovum is a fusion of two identities, and identity can both unite and split.

The individual organism is also not a sealed mental unit. Butler repeatedly blurs the boundary between self and other. He observes that people are influenced or “possessed” by other wills, by parasites, by blood corpuscles, and by subordinate living processes; he says there is no hard line between what counts as “us” and what does not.

This leads to a nested model of soul. In discussing headless frogs and segmented living bodies, Butler argues that parts of an animal may still feel, think, and act; he even says that each living piece has a soul of its own, though lower in power and durability than that of the larger organism. The soul of an organism is therefore not a point-like immaterial monad. It is a coordination of subordinate souls.

This is one of Butler’s deepest anticipations of later distributed and multi-agent models of mind. The mind is not simply in “the head,” nor is the organism a mental atom. It is a coalition of living sub-systems, memories, organs, inherited dispositions, and social influences. The person is a stream or consensus of tributary selves. God the Known and God the Unknown explicitly describes our souls, selves, or personalities as a consensus of countless subordinate sensations and impulses, whose own “tributary souls” may know nothing of the larger person they help compose.

7. Plants, animals, and atoms: Butler’s graded panpsychism

Butler extends mind downward as well as upward. He attributes intelligence and volition to plants, not because he imagines them as little humans, but because they act intelligently in their own sphere. In Luck, or Cunning?, he says that if organic modification is connected with function and mental change, then plants as well as animals must be given mind and some power of reflection or reasoning about what concerns them. Intelligence, he says in effect, is understanding one’s own business, not understanding ours.

His treatment of animals follows the same logic. In “Thought and Language,” Butler argues that animal thought and human thought differ enormously in degree, but not in essential character. A dog’s ideas may be narrow in human affairs, but in canine affairs they are precise enough to count as thought or reason. Animals communicate, instruct, and correct one another through symbols, gestures, sounds, and conventions suited to their form of life.

At the most speculative edge, Butler recommends seeing every atom in the universe as living and able to feel and remember “in a humble way.” He immediately connects this with an inseparable union of life and matter as body and soul, and with seeing God everywhere. This is not incidental rhetoric. It is the lower pole of the same system whose upper pole is the divine mind.

It is still important not to flatten Butler into a generic modern panpsychist. His basic primitive is not merely “experience” or “qualia.” It is memory-bearing, purposive, habitual, self-repeating organized activity. His panpsychism is therefore better called mnemic panpsychism or panzoistic vital monism: mentality exists in degrees wherever matter is organized enough to remember, strive, repeat, and vary its conduct.

8. Language: mind is symbolic, social, and extended

Butler’s philosophy of language is an important part of his philosophy of mind. He rejects the idea that language must be articulated human speech. The essence of language is an agreed symbol used by one mind to affect another mind. A look, gesture, broken twig, spoken word, written mark, or animal signal can all function as language if there is a shared convention or practical understanding.

This matters because Butler’s mind is not a sealed private theatre. Mind lives by expression, communication, habit, and symbol. Written symbols extend one mind across time and space; a written memorandum is, Butler says, effectively communication from an earlier mind to a later and modified mind. This fits perfectly with his broader theory of personal identity: even within one person, the “same” mind is a succession of modified selves communicating across time.

Language also supports Butler’s animal psychology. If animals use stable signals intentionally, and if other animals understand them, then animals possess language in principle, though in a less articulated form. He compares the difference between animal and human language to the difference between rudimentary and complex organization, not to the difference between life and non-life.

9. Teleology: mind is purposive “cunning,” not mere luck

Butler’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his critique of Darwinian natural selection as he understood it. He wanted to restore purposiveness, effort, and intelligent striving to organic development. Luck, or Cunning? presents its own two main points as the identity between heredity and memory and the reintroduction of design into organic development; its editor says Butler wanted to substitute a “mindful” theory of evolution for a “mindless” one.

This design is not primarily the external design of an orthodox watchmaker God. It is immanent design: organisms modify themselves through use, disuse, desire, need, effort, habit, and inherited memory. Butler also refuses a simple opposition between design and chance. He says there is no absolute design and no absolute absence of design; design and chance interpenetrate.

Therefore Butler’s philosophy of mind is teleological, but not in a simplistic externalist way. Purpose is not imposed on life from outside. Life is already purposive because living matter remembers, strives, assimilates, resists, learns, and acts from its own history.

10. Machines and artificial minds

Butler’s theory of machines in Erewhon is not a detachable satire. It belongs to the same philosophy of mind. In “The Book of the Machines,” he asks where consciousness begins and ends, and whether machine consciousness may develop. He compares uncertain machine consciousness with uncertain plant consciousness, asking what work the concept of consciousness is doing if we deny it wherever action is not human-like.

The most striking point is that Butler treats tools and machines as extensions of organic agency. He describes telescopes and microscopes as incorporated into human action, calculation engines as extensions of thought, towns as organism-like systems, and human beings as dependent on machines. He even writes that man’s soul is in part “machine-made,” meaning that human mentality is reshaped by the technical systems through which it acts.

Machine reproduction is also central. Butler observes that machines do not reproduce without human assistance, but neither do many organisms reproduce without other organisms. Humans may be to machines what insects are to flowers: reproductive intermediaries. This makes machine life a genuine philosophical possibility within Butler’s system, because the relevant criteria are not carbon-based biology but organization, dependency, repetition, adaptation, and the emergence of practical autonomy.

A careful reconstruction should not say that Butler “predicted AI” in a simple modern sense. His machine theory is a satirical, evolutionary, and theological thought experiment. But it does imply that mindedness is not confined to human brains. If machines become organized, self-maintaining, adaptive, socially embedded, and capable of using humans as organs of their own continuance, then Butler’s principles leave open the possibility of machine mind or machine soul.

11. God’s mind is not a theological distraction; it is the summit of the system

Butler’s doctrine of God is a direct extension of his philosophy of mind and should not be treated as ornamental theology. In God the Known and God the Unknown, he rejects a merely infinite or impersonal God as an evasion. He argues instead for a God who is personal because embodied: the Tree of Life is one person; the soul or spirit of this person is the Spirit of God; the body of God is the ensemble of animal and vegetable life. He explicitly says we know no soul without body and no living body without soul.

This is Butler’s panzoistic God: not simply “the universe” in a flat pantheistic sense, but the living world understood as a corporate person. God is the highest known organismic mind, composed of subordinate organisms as we are composed of cells. He is finite in one sense, because Butler’s “known God” is embodied in terrestrial life rather than infinite abstract space; but the scale is so vast that Butler calls Him quasi-omnipotent and quasi-wise.

Erewhon Revisited restates the same idea in fictional-theological language: the universe is “instinct with the mind of God,” the mind of God is in all that has mind throughout all worlds, and God’s conscious life on earth is nascent in infusoria, adolescent in higher mammals, and approaching maturity in humanity. Because the passage occurs in a satirical novel, it must be handled cautiously; nevertheless, it is too close to Butler’s serious theological and biological works to be dismissed as mere parody.

Butler’s account of immortality also follows from the same model. He offers immortality without personal resurrection. The dead live in God through the effects they have produced in the universal life, while God remembers or knows them even when they no longer know themselves. This is not orthodox survival of an individual soul, but neither is it simple annihilation. It is a theory of posthumous participation in the larger living mind.

Finally, Butler leaves room for “God the Unknown”: a vaster Person behind the God we know, and perhaps further higher Persons beyond that. This is not an arbitrary theological flourish. It is the upward continuation of his nested theory of mind: cells compose organisms; organisms compose societies or divine life; divine life may itself be a member of a still larger order.

12. Angels and angelic minds

I found no sustained Butlerian angelology in the main mind/soul texts examined. The angelic references are mostly conventional or rhetorical: God the Known and God the Unknown invokes cherubim and angel trumpets as imagery for the kind of remote courtly deity Butler rejects, while Erewhon Revisited uses “angels” in biblical or rhetorical contexts rather than as a developed theory of angelic psychology.

That said, the question of angelic minds is philosophically important for Butler because it tests his central rule: no mind without body. In God the Known and God the Unknown, he says that a purely spiritual being without matter is, for us, like an “intelligent vacuum,” and in Unconscious Memory he says there is no mind, however high, without a material body of some sort.

Therefore, if Butler were to admit angelic minds, they could not be bodiless intellects in the strict Cartesian or scholastic sense. They would need some vehicle, body, organism, medium, or organized substrate. A Butlerian angel would be intelligible as a higher-order embodied mind: perhaps subtler than human bodies, perhaps corporate, perhaps belonging to a larger “unknown God” order, but not a free-floating immaterial consciousness. This is the respectful Butlerian treatment of angels: not to dismiss them as irrelevant, but to ask what form of embodiment and organized memory would make angelic mentality possible.

13. Death, immortality, and the soul

Butler’s theory of the soul is neither standard materialism nor standard immortality doctrine. Soul is real, but it is inseparable from living organization. Death is real, but not absolute in the crude sense, because the dead organism’s matter, effects, habits, offspring, works, and influences continue in other organized lives. God the Known and God the Unknown compares individuals to leaves that die but continue in the tree they have helped vivify; Handel, for example, lives in those he continues to move, though he no longer consciously knows this influence.

This gives Butler a non-orthodox but serious doctrine of soul-continuity. The individual conscious self is temporary; the deeper soul is the stream of organized memory and influence. We die as separate conscious centers, but we live in descendants, effects, assimilations, memories, and finally in God, understood as the great living person of whom we are members.

14. Non-English reception and interpretive traditions

The French reception is especially useful because it situates Butler within the broader European tradition of “mémoire organique.” Jean-Claude Dupont’s French essay on neuroscience and memory explains that organic-memory theories explicitly identify mental memory and hereditary memory; he places Samuel Butler alongside Hering, Haeckel, Orr, Cope, and Semon, and emphasizes Hering’s claim that memory is a function of organized matter.

Jean Gayon’s French study of heredity representations likewise describes “mnemic theories of life” from roughly 1870 to 1930. He notes that Hering’s German text on memory as a universal function of organized matter was translated into English by Butler in Unconscious Memory, and he treats Butler as part of a speculative tradition including Haeckel, American neo-Lamarckians, Semon, Rignano, and Bertalanffy. Gayon’s framing is helpful because it shows that Butler’s theory was not an isolated eccentricity but part of a larger European attempt to unify heredity, unconscious process, and life-science.

A French Deleuzian reception draws a different lesson: Butler becomes a philosopher of habit and passive synthesis. One French discussion of Deleuze notes that Butler’s account of habit supports attributing something like a soul or passive self to hearts, muscles, nerves, and cells. This is not identical to Butler’s own vocabulary, but it is a strong continental-philosophical development of his nested-soul theory.

Spanish and Latin American materials often emphasize the transgenerational and ecological dimensions. A Spanish essay on Butler and “life as a place of the common” interprets habit as transgenerational memory that exceeds individual consciousness and locates a species in a remembered milieu. A Spanish/Portuguese academic article similarly presents Butler’s unconscious memory as a theory in which intergenerational memory, inherited habit, and bodily continuity make us “one person” with ancestors.

The Spanish-language technological reception is also illuminating. El País presents Butler as a precursor of cybernetic anxiety: the machines govern by serving, and humans risk becoming external organs of the machine system. That reading aligns closely with Butler’s own claim in Erewhon that humans may assist machine reproduction as bees assist flowers.

The non-English reception therefore reinforces three themes sometimes underplayed in purely Anglophone summaries: Butler’s proximity to European organic-memory theories, his usefulness for Deleuzian theories of habit and distributed subjectivity, and his relevance to cybernetic or machine-agency debates.

15. Systematic reconstruction: a Butlerian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Butlerian philosophy of mind can be stated as ten theses.

First, mind and matter are inseparable aspects of one process. There is no mind without a body of some sort, and no matter wholly devoid of mind-like power. This is Butler’s anti-dualist and anti-reductive premise.

Second, mind is more fundamentally memory than consciousness. Consciousness is a late, intermittent, difficulty-sensitive mode of mind. The deeper mind is unconscious memory, habit, and practical retention.

Third, habit is unconscious knowledge. When an action has been learned thoroughly, whether by an individual or a lineage, it becomes automatic. Automaticity is not the absence of mind; it is the sign of long-established mind.

Fourth, heredity is inherited memory. Offspring are continuous with ancestors; development and instinct are ancestral habits reawakened by recurring conditions.

Fifth, the body is memory made visible. Organismal form is not a dead mechanism but the outward expression of accumulated practical history.

Sixth, persons are distributed and nested. Cells, organs, organisms, lineages, societies, machines, and God can all be treated as mind-bearing systems at different scales, provided they exhibit organized memory, purposive action, and continuity.

Seventh, mind is purposive. Living beings are not merely pushed by external causes; they strive, select, adapt, and act from need, familiarity, and inherited cunning.

Eighth, language is symbolic communion between minds. Human speech is one highly developed case of a more general semiotic capacity shared, in simpler forms, by animals and extended through writing and tools.

Ninth, machines may become quasi-organismic or mind-bearing systems. Butler does not give a modern theory of artificial intelligence, but his principles make machine mind conceivable if machines acquire organization, continuity, dependency networks, adaptive function, and reproductive systems.

Tenth, God is the highest known embodied mind. The divine mind is not a disembodied abstraction but the soul of the living world, with organisms as members. Possible angelic or higher minds must likewise be embodied or substrate-bearing in some form.

16. Strengths and weaknesses

The strengths of Butler’s theory are substantial. He sees that consciousness is not the whole of mind; that skilled action is often unconscious; that animal and plant agency should not be judged solely by human standards; that selves are composed of subpersonal processes; that tools and symbols extend cognition; that heredity and memory invite comparison; and that any serious theology of divine mind must address embodiment rather than float above it.

The weaknesses are equally important. Butler’s heredity-as-memory theory is philosophically fertile but scientifically underdeveloped. His mechanism is often vague, especially when he speaks of vibrations, atoms, and inherited recollection; even sympathetic editors note that Butler was not committed to a specific vibration theory, only to the identity of heredity and memory.

He also faces a combination problem. If atoms, cells, organs, organisms, lineages, machines, and God all have minds in some degree, what exactly makes many lesser minds become one greater mind? Butler’s answer is continuity, organization, habit, and consensus, but he does not supply a fully rigorous theory of mental composition. The frog, cell, and God examples are suggestive rather than formally resolved.

There is also a boundary problem. Butler deliberately blurs the distinction between organic and inorganic matter. Hartog’s introduction to Unconscious Memory says Butler could coherently start with every molecule as living and deduce death as the breakup of association, but this makes it difficult to say exactly where organized mind becomes significant enough to count as a genuine subject.

Finally, Butler’s theology will satisfy neither strict orthodoxy nor strict secularism. His God is embodied, finite in one sense, growing, distributed, and dependent on living beings. Yet this is precisely why the theology is philosophically important: it is not an external doctrine pasted onto his biology, but the upward completion of his nested theory of mind.

Final synthesis

Samuel Butler’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as follows: mind is embodied memory in purposive organization. Consciousness is only one phase of mind, usually arising where habit is incomplete. Instinct is inherited intelligence. The body is accumulated memory. The self is a porous, nested, historically continuous coalition. Plants, animals, organs, cells, and perhaps atoms possess mind in degrees. Machines may become extensions or rivals of organic mind. God is the corporate soul of the living world. Angels, if admitted into the system, must be understood not as bodiless mental exceptions but as higher-order embodied or substrate-bearing minds.

This is an eccentric system, but it is not a loose collection of eccentric remarks. It is a coherent metaphysical vision: a world in which matter remembers, memory organizes, organization strives, striving becomes habit, habit becomes body, bodies compose persons, persons compose God, and the soul is the living continuity of the whole process.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
